text: The surgeon introduces a liver retractor to elevate the left lobe of the liver, followed by the dissection of adhesions between the stomach and the left lobe of the liver using laparoscopic instruments.
The surgeon carefully dissects between the previous Nissen fundoplication and the left lobe of the liver, maintaining proximity to the liver to prevent injury to the stomach.
The surgeon carefully dissects between the nissen wrap and the underlying esophagus using a combination of blunt and sharp dissection techniques, navigating a challenging area of the procedure.

The surgeon identifies the nascent wrap at the superior aspect, with a nasogastric tube visible in the esophagus to aid in identification during dissection. The tube is adjusted by anesthesia as the dissection progresses.

The surgeon dissects adhesions between the diaphragm and the nissen fundoplication, creating an adequate window. A nasogastric tube is visible in the stomach. Once the window is obtained, the nissen wrap is transected using a green load stapler.
The surgeon carefully avoids clips from the previous surgery to prevent stapler misfire, then proceeds to dissect the angle of His after transecting the nascent wrap.
The surgeon carefully dissects and removes adhesions from the previous surgery, first anteriorly and then posteriorly, using blunt and sharp techniques to free the tissue.

The surgeon creates a gastric pouch on the lesser curvature by transecting the lesser omentum using a blue load stapler in a perigastric approach. Adhesions in the posterior aspect of the stomach are dissected, revealing adhesions from a previous surgery. The angle of His is defined from the posterior aspect of the gastric pouch, with additional transection of adhesions.
The surgeon transects the remaining portion of the gastric pouch using a green load stapler, chosen due to the increased tissue thickness from previous surgeries, after obtaining an adequate window.

The surgeon places clips for additional security after creating the gastric pouch. The transverse colon and mesocolon are elevated to locate the ligament of Treitz, exposing the inferior mesenteric vein and the pancreas.
The surgeon measures approximately forty centimeters distally from the ligament of Treitz and transects the small bowel using a white load vascular stapler.
The surgeon places a clip on the biliopancreatic limb to confirm its identity, then runs the limb back to the ligamentary tracts, ensuring its correct positioning.
The surgeon elevates the alimentary limb to the gastric pouch and begins constructing a two-layer gastro-jejunal anastomosis. The posterior layer is being sutured using Vicryl stitches.

After completing the posterior layer of the anastomosis, the surgeon creates a gastrotomy in the gastric pouch and an entrotomy in the alimentary limb using a Harmonic Scalpel. A nasogastric tube is used to assist in the creation of the anastomosis, followed by a second layer of stitching with Vicryl sutures.

The surgeon completes the second posterior layer of the gastro-jejunal anastomosis, taking separate stitches at the corners for secure bites. A 32 French tube is passed into the small bowel to calibrate the anastomosis, ensuring the posterior layer is not compromised. The third layer is completed, followed by a fourth layer using 2-0 Vicryl stitches.

The surgeon tests the anastomosis by submerging it underwater and insufflating air to check for leaks. After confirming the integrity, the surgeon counts the alimentary limb to approximately one hundred centimeters to prepare for the creation of the jejunal jejunostomy.

The surgeon places an anchor stitch between the alimentary limb and the biliopancreatic limb, followed by creating an enterotomy in each limb. An anastomosis is performed using a 60mm white load stapler.
The surgeon closes the infratomy using two micro-stitchers, followed by closing the mesenteric defects with silk sutures. The closure is performed in segments to carefully avoid mesenteric vessels.

The surgeon uses silk sutures to completely close the mesenteric defect between the bowel loops and the mesentery. Following this, the jejuno-jejunostomy is completed, and a final endoscopy is performed. The anastomosis is submerged underwater to check for leaks and ensure patency, concluding the procedure.
